coredata.dc:title: Controlled reaction dynamics of cellulose: Revealing the dual morphology nanocrystals
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Joseph, Sicily R.
Janardhanan, Jith C.
Padmanabhan, Nisha T.
John, Honey
Mythili, Ushamani
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose nanocrystals exhibit diverse morphology with embedded crystallinity derived from the ordered crystalline regimes of cellulose fibers. Acid hydrolysis parameters controls the morphology of nanocrystals and is the primary efficient route for the synthesis of cellulose nanocrystals. In this study, we explored the impact of temperature on the morphology engineering of cellulose nanocrystals derived from banana pseudostem by oxalic acid hydrolysis, an underexplored method. Confirmation of successful cellulose extraction was achieved through fibrous morphology observed in FESEM and TEM analyses. X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy affirmed the presence of type II cellulose. Notably, for the first time, we report the derivation of toroidal and spherulitic sheet-like morphologies in cellulose nanocrystals synthesized by hydrothermal method from banana psuedostem. Our investigation revealed that cellulose hydrolysis at 120 °C yields nanocrystals characterized by toroidal and spherulitic sheet-like morphologies with excellent crystallinity, as confirmed by FESEM and TEM analyses, with average thicknesses of 13.70 nm and 18.05 nm, respectively. Interestingly, elevating the hydrolysis temperature to 180 °C led to the collapse of these structures into carbon dots, exhibiting notable cyan emission.

JCJ is grateful to Rashtriya Uchchatar Shiksha Abhiyan (RUSA) Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the preparation of cellulose nanocrystals from outer banana pseudostem.
XRD pattern (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of extracted cellulose (BD) and cellulose nanocrystals (BCNC).
FESEM image depicting toroidal (a) and sheet like (b) morphology of BCNC, TEM image depicting torroidal (c, d) and sheet like (f, g) morphology of BCNC and corresponding SAED pattern (e, h).
C1s and O1s XPS spectra of extracted cellulose (a, c) and cellulose nanocrystals (b, d).
Possible mechanism for morphological evolution from fibrous cellulose to cellulose nanocrystals.
UV spectra (a) PL spectra (b) TEM image (c)TEM image at higher magnification (d) and SAED pattern (e) of synthesized carbon dot.

head.ce:title: Controlled reaction dynamics of cellulose: Revealing the dual morphology nanocrystals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose nanocrystals exhibit diverse morphology with embedded crystallinity derived from the ordered crystalline regimes of cellulose fibers. Acid hydrolysis parameters controls the morphology of nanocrystals and is the primary efficient route for the synthesis of cellulose nanocrystals. In this study, we explored the impact of temperature on the morphology engineering of cellulose nanocrystals derived from banana pseudostem by oxalic acid hydrolysis, an underexplored method. Confirmation of successful cellulose extraction was achieved through fibrous morphology observed in FESEM and TEM analyses. X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy affirmed the presence of type II cellulose. Notably, for the first time, we report the derivation of toroidal and spherulitic sheet-like morphologies in cellulose nanocrystals synthesized by hydrothermal method from banana psuedostem. Our investigation revealed that cellulose hydrolysis at 120 °C yields nanocrystals characterized by toroidal and spherulitic sheet-like morphologies with excellent crystallinity, as confirmed by FESEM and TEM analyses, with average thicknesses of 13.70 nm and 18.05 nm, respectively. Interestingly, elevating the hydrolysis temperature to 180 °C led to the collapse of these structures into carbon dots, exhibiting notable cyan emission.

Engineering of cellulose nanocrystals to toroidal and spherulitic sheet-like morphology via oxalic acid hydrolysis of banana pseudostem under hydrothermal conditions in which the temperature variation led to quantum dots by structure collapse.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are cellulose nanoparticles with high aspect ratio and crystalline regimes due to the well-ordered crystalline structures. Since 2017, the term CNC has been collectively used to describe various cellulose-based materials, including nanowhiskers, microcrystallites, nanowires, nano rods, cellulose sols, etc. [1] Their nano-dimension combined with excellent aspect ratio, colloidal stability, and crystallinity index, have made CNCs a highly sought-after material with widespread applications in diverse scientific areas like energy harvesting/storage, biomedicals, magneto-electronics, 3D-printing, electronics, cosmetics, food packaging and other commercial applications [2]. Morphology of CNC has a profound impact on the properties of CNCs and apart from the general categorization of nanorods/nanospheres, CNCs exhibits other morphologies like nano needles, ribbons and porous networks [3–6]. The selected source of cellulose, acid hydrolysis parameters critically controlling the morphology of CNCs, and the intricate dynamics involved in the formation of CNCs pose a considerable challenge in achieving precise control over their morphology [7].

Considering sustainability and environmental considerations, the production of CNC from agro-industry waste offers a distinct advantage over other sources. Banana pseudostem is the major by-product of agro-industry generated at roughly 220 tons per hectare of banana cultivation and is an abundant source of natural cellulose, along with more than 50% of cellulose of the total composition making it an ideal candidate for synthesizing CNC [8]. Acid hydrolysis, high-pressure mechanical homogenization, enzymatic hydrolysis and TEMPO-mediated oxidation are the usually employed routes for preparing CNCs from banana pseudostem among which the primary standard route is acid hydrolysis [9].

Acid hydrolysis parameters, i.e. concentration and temperature have a crucial dominant role in the morphology control of CNCs [10]. Generally, mineral acids such as sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, and phosphoric acid are employed for the hydrolysis which specifically degrades the less ordered amorphous regions while keeping the crystalline phases intact [11]. The hydronium (H3O+) ions promotes the hydrolysis of amorphous cellulose by protonating the glycosidic linkages. Recently, the combination of strong mineral acid and weak organic acid was employed for the hydrolysis of cellulose fibres to CNCs with needle-like morphology [12]. However tuning CNC's morphology by hydrolyzing the cellulose fibres, solely with weak organic acid is a challenging and less explored endeavor [12,13]. In this work, we have reported CNCs with mixed toroid and spherulitic sheet-like morphology by the hydrolysis of the outer part of banana pseudostem by using oxalic acid at a temperature of 120OC. The morphology mixture has excellent colloidal stability and crystallinity index which can be attributed to surface terminations and well-ordered crystallites arrangement. The temperature has a crucial role and the structures are collapsed to carbon dots with impressive cyan emission upon elevating the hydrolysis temperature to 180 OC.

The synthesis of cellulose nanocrystals from the banana pseudo stem is shown in Fig. 1 . What sets this apart from other methods is the of the outer part of banana pseudo stem which is less employed for cellulose extraction in comparison with the inner core. CNCs were synthesized from powdered pseudo stem (BR) via double bleaching (BB) and hydroxide hydrolysis (BN), DMSO treatment to obtain cellulose (BD) [12]. The extraction of cellulose is followed by hydrothermal treatment in the presence of oxalic acid at 120 OC for 6 h (Figure S1).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to differentiate the native and treated fibers (Figure S2). The untreated material showed distinct peaks at 2θ values of 16°, 22°, and 35° corresponding to (110), (200), and (004) lattice planes respectively, depicting cellulose type I [12]. Additional peaks at 2θ values of 15°, 24.5°, and 30.0° in the BR sample is likely to represent non-cellulosic substances. Post-treatment diffraction peaks for the BN and BD samples were observed around 12°, 20°, and 22° indicative of a transformation of type-I to cellulose type II due to mercerization with NaOH, correlating respectively to the (100), (110), and (020) planes [12]. During mercerization, sodium ions (Na+) penetrate the intracrystalline regions of cellulose inducing the swelling and disrupting the existing intermolecular hydrogen bonds. This results in a permanent structural alteration wherein the initial parallel arrangement of chains in cellulose I undergoes an irreversibly conversion to antiparallel cellulose-II [12]. (Figure S3a). Cellulose, in its two forms, I and II, exhibits a multitude of hydrogen bonds, encompassing both intermolecular and intramolecular interactions. In cellulose I, there exist intermolecular bonds, denoted O (6)H–O (3′) alongside intramolecular hydrogen bonds, such as 2(OH)–O-6 and O (3)H–O (Figure S3b). On the other hand, cellulose II not only retains the above bonds, but also introduces supplementary intermolecular hydrogen bonds, specifically O (2)H–O (2) and O (6)H–O (2’) accounting for its higher stability compared to cellulose-I. Furthermore, cellulose II allows for the exposure and accessibility of specific hydroxyl groups that were previously unattainable in cellulose I [13]. FT-IR spectra revealed that the essential chemical structure of cellulose fiber remains intact even after undergoing extensive chemical treatments (Figure S4). The broad peak at 3319, 2917 cm⁻1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of surface hydroxyl moieties comprising both intra, intermolecular hydrogen bonding and asymmetric stretching vibration of aliphatic saturated –CH moieties in cellulose respectively. Contribution from lignin and hemicellulose moieties is diminishing after each stage of chemical treatment for cellulose extraction [14]. FESEM investigations, further complements FT-IR and XRD analyses, disclosing the removal of cementing substances and the liberation of cellulose fibers following each phase of chemical treatment applied to the raw materials. (Figure S5).

BD can be transformed into BCNCs under hydrothermal conditions. The XRD pattern of BCNC (Fig. 2 a), revealed characteristic diffraction peaks at angles of 12°, 20°, and 22 indicatives of cellulose-II's presence in BCNC [12]. The presence of cellulose II was further confirmed via Raman spectra (Figure S6). In the Raman spectroscopic analysis distinct peaks were observed at 353 cm−1, 577 cm−1, and 1462 cm−1, indicative of the presence of type-II cellulose [15]. Notably, the peak at 577 cm−1 is exclusive characteristics of cellulose II, and is reportedly absent in the Raman spectra of cellulose I and amorphous cellulose [15]. Both XRD and Raman peaks of BCNC exhibit sharp peaks and this can be ascribed to an enhanced crystallinity of the cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) and the removal of non-cellulosic components from the CNCs following acid hydrolysis. The FT-IR spectrum of BCNCs closely resembles BD, yet is characterized by more distinct, sharper bands. A notable shift is observed in the glycosidic linkage which is observed at 885 cm−1 for BD and at 894 cm−1 for BCNC (Fig. 2b) revealing the transition from cellulose I to cellulose II [16].

Successful extraction of cellulose was confirmed by fibrous morphology in FESEM (Figure S5 d) and TEM (Figure S7a) analysis. Subsequently, under hydrothermal conditions, these fibers transformed into BCNCs exhibiting a remarkable morphological diversity. The most striking feature observed in FESEM (Fig. 3 a) and TEM (Fig. 3c and d) was the toroidal form of BCNC, a ring-like structure contrasting with typical needle-like, rod-like morphologies or ribbon like common morphologies reported in literature [3,4]. Additionally, sheet-like configurations (Fig. 3b and. f,g) suggested a spherulitic growth pattern, further diversifying the morphology. The average dimensions of toroidal morphology were quantified, revealing an inner diameter of 6.4 nm and an outer diameter of 20.1 nm. The measured thickness of the toroid and sheet were determined to be 13.7 nm and 18.1 nm, respectively (Figure S8).The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of BCNC exhibits a distinctive series of bright spots, indicative of diffraction from the crystallites of nanocrystals. Notably, the absence of diffuse rings within the pattern implies the lack of amorphous regions in the BCNC structure. SAED analysis revealed a transition from low to high crystallinity from cellulose fibers (Figure S7b) to BCNC (Fig. 3e–h). Specifically, SAED patterns of toroidal BCNC displayed diffraction spots corresponding to the (020) and (110) planes, while those of sheet-like BCNC primarily showed spots associated with the (110) plane. Remarkably, these plane orientations observed in the SAED patterns correlate with the planes obtained from X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis.

XPS analysis provide conclusive evidence for the morphology transition from fibres to nanocrystals (Fig. 4 (a–d)). A visible change is evident in C1s, O 1s peak profile from nanofibers to nanocrystals. In the deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of nanofibers, contribution from C–C, C–OH, -O-C-O moieties at binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.7 eV and 288.2 eV, respectively were observed in the order -O-C-O > -C-OH > C–C. As moving to nanocrystals, the contribution from the -C-OH moiety become prominent and blue-shifts were observed in the binding energies of C–OH and -C-O-C moieties at 284.1 eV and 282.3 eV, respectively. The peak profile change (Table S1) and binding energy shift (Table S2) can be attributed to the cleavage of the terminal end of cellulose chains and strong hydrogen bonded networks [17]. O 1s deconvoluted spectrum also supported the cleavage of terminal cellulose chains [18].

The glycosidic linkages at these terminals, in contrast to core segments are more accessible to oxalic acid and oxalic acid selectively hydrolyzes the β(1 → 4) glycosidic bonds of cellulose II chains, leading to the release of glucose monomers or small oligosaccharides from these ends. Since the core segments are fortified by a robust matrix of hydrogen bonds, compared to terminal ends, the core provides initial resistance to hydrolysis (Fig. 5 ) As hydrolysis progresses, the inherent stresses in the polymer chains favors the coiling of fibrous core structure. This coiling leads to the formation of toroidal structures as hydroxyl groups at the ends of intact chains interact by hydrogen bonds and facilitates a self-coiling mechanism. Simultaneously, the cleaved terminal ends undergo self-assembly through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions in an ordered overlapping fashion akin to the sheets seen in spherulite [19,20].

Very interestingly it was observed that cellulose modified into cyan emissive CDs upon hydrothermal treatment of cellulose with 50% oxalic acid at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h (Figure S9). The TEM images of CDs (Fig. 6 c) reveal uniform and well dispersed array of spherical shaped CDs with an average size of 3.15 nm as obtained from the TEM micrograph [21]. The fringe width of the carbon dot (Fig. 6d), was determined to be 0.15 nm, corresponding to the 103 plane (PDF Card Number-00-023-0064). Further insights from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the carbon dots (Fig. 6e) disclose a diffuse ring pattern, indicative of the poor crystallinity of the carbon dots. This attenuated crystalline nature is conjectured to result from the rich oxygen-containing functional groups within the structure [22]. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 6a) exhibit two distinct absorption peaks at 275 and 350 nm with sharp and broad nature which can be assigned to π→π* and n→π* electronic transition intrinsic to CD surface [22,21].Also the CDs exhibits excitation independent fluorescence emission and when excited from 260 to 360 nm, the CDs exhibit emission centered around 388 nm (Fig. 6b). [22] When the solution is excited at a wavelength of 360 nm, emission can be collected from 370 nm which results in the appearance of a seemingly incomplete line at 360 nm.

We have explored the morphology engineering of cellulose nanocrystals from banana pseudostem using oxalic acid hydrolysis, and reported toroidal morphology of cellulose nanocrystals. Hydrolysis temperature is a key factor and at a temperature of 120 °C, we observed the emergence of CNCs with distinctive toroidal and spherulitic sheet-like shapes, along with excellent crystallinity. Intriguingly, when the hydrolysis temperature is increased to 180 °C, these structures collapsed to carbon dots with cyan fluorescence. The ability to control and manipulate the morphology of CNCs, as demonstrated in this study, is pivotal in expanding their functionality and enhancing their performance in diverse applications.

Sicily R. Joseph: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jith C. Janardhanan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Nisha T. Padmanabhan: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Honey John: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ushamani Mythili: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors used Chatgpt and Quilbot in order to improve the language. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and takes full responsibility for the content of the publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SRJ acknowledges DST-SAIF Cochin, instrumentation support of CLIF, DST-SAIF Kottayam, Teresian Instrumentation and Consultancy Centre(TICC) and Amrita Centre for Nanosciences and Molecular Medicine (ACNSMM) for their support in doing various characterisations. JCJ is grateful to Rashtriya Uchchatar Shiksha Abhiyan (RUSA) Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT).
